text: Hi, I’m Arjun — thanks for making the time today. This is a short, recorded AI screening for the Jr. Forward Deployed Engineer role, and it’s about building and operating AI-driven integrations and automations on Workato across enterprise and cloud apps. Let’s get started, shall we?

You’re building a Workato recipe that calls an AI to auto-triage IT tickets. How would you design the flow so the AI’s decision reliably routes the ticket?

So can you tell me more give me some more context, like, what are these tickets? Like, are these coming from something like Jira? Like, who are the, like, what end consumers who are to whom we are routing these to guys to.
Sure —

moving on — say the tickets are coming from Jira and ServiceNow for an internal IT helpdesk, and the workflow is supposed to decide whether to route them to the network, app support, or security queue. How would you make that routing reliable?

So, like, I designed this workflow as a reliable human in loop system instead of fully autonomous routing. The recipe would start whenever a ticket ID new IT ticket is very created on Jira. So, like, first, right, normalize and sanitize the input data to extract things like type description,
logs, tags, severity, and any other metadata.
Then the Workato would call the LLM using a structured prompt,
that would then classify the ticket into different categories like issue categories,
or IT networking team and things like that. I would use, like, something like confidence threshold for reliability.

Like, for example, I need the confidence of the LLM is, like, above percent, it can go to directly be order of it. But, like, anything between, let's say, seventy to ninety eight percent, human approval is required. And, like, for something any confidence that is below seventy percent, a med wall triage is, uh, required.
So so I I that's how I think I will will will it.
Got it — human-in-loop routing…

what parameter it had, the timestamps, and, like, the reasoning somebody from directly from the

You’re building a custom connector to a REST API that enforces rate limits. How would you design around the limit to avoid dropped calls?
What are the kind of rate limits we're dealing with you? Okay.
Can give me some examples.
Right —
Say it’s a connector syncing ticket updates to a SaaS A-P-I that returns four twenty-nines after a fixed number of calls per minute. How would you design around the limit to avoid dropped calls?

So, like, I always design the connectors with, like, rate limit in my mind. So, like, the connectors are read are read away from the start rather than, like, instead of, like, reacting it after a failure happens. So, well, the first thing I would do is understand the provider's limit for the API. For example, like, in your in this case, for example, it's a permanent rate limit. Then I would introduce throw introduce something like throttle inside the connectors or request space within a safe threshold instead of hitting that limit? For something like errors like four twenty nine? I would implement I would, like, add response triggers like exponential backoffs,
with jitters.
I see — throttling and backoff…
How would you ensure idempotency on retries?
So yeah. So sure. So I can make sure item is properly I'd use, like, idempotency keys so that retries don't care don't create any duplicate. Duplicates during any kind of post operations.
Even if if it's during the day tries and everything, So we keep on track, so we are not building any including any duplicates.

In whichever of Java, Python, or Ruby you’re strongest, how would you implement a robust JSON transform that validates and maps fields for an integration step?

Sure. So, like, have most of my life, I've been working in Python, so I'll answer with Python. So in Python, like, I instead of building just one function, I'd separate this into, like, three different separate functions. So, like, forward validation, transformation, and mapping logic. Separating this helps us with the maintainability.
And the ability of everything. So, like, first, I I'd validate the incoming JSON against expected schema to ensure required field success and the data types are correct.
Then I would like map source fields into the target using transformation.

Transformation functions instead of, like, hardcore environment. For example, data normalization, you know, mapping, nested field flattening, and then setting default values for missing fields. Then I'll also handle, like, invalid payload gracefully using structured exceptions rather than fillings, like, silently. And, like, I do use, type models or serializers where possible to reduce runtime issues. And, like, at the end, like, just like everything else, I'd like to do proper locking and unit testing to validate mapping behavior
across edge cases.
Got it — Python, validation and mapping…
Tell me about a time you led an integration project and had to align cross-functional teams on requirements.

Sure. So, like, one integration project and there involved connecting multiple systems that different teams depended on. For operational workflow. The challenge was the engineering on operation and the business stakeholders.

All had, like, slightly different expectations around the how the data should move and what the success criteria work. So my role was to first align requirement and ensure the integration is all the actual operational problem instead of just moving data between systems. I started by running requirement gathering sessions with each stakeholder group. Dependencies, failures, and Then I documented the integration flow API contract. So pay load structure and then ownership boundaries. So everyone had a shared understanding during implementation I schedule checkpoints with stakeholders to validate the assumption and avoid late surprises. Uh, as a result, uh, we delivered the with fewer production issues and significantly reduced my operation work.
Okay — cross-functional alignment…
I think that gives me a good picture of things, ya. Thanks so much for your time today — the recruitment team will be in touch with you about the next steps.
